title: Geometry: Area and Volume
blurb: Find areas of triangles, parallelograms, trapezoids, and composite figures; put polygons on the coordinate plane; fill and wrap prisms with volume and surface area.

lessons.title: Area of Rectangles & Parallelograms
lessons.concreteComparison: Tiling a kitchen floor with 1-meter square tiles, you don't count 35 tiles one by one — you see 7 rows of 5 and multiply. Every area formula in this topic is that same shortcut: a fast way to count unit squares without counting.
lessons.objective: Find areas of rectangles and parallelograms using $A = lw$ and $A = bh$, including fractional side lengths.
concept: **Area** measures the space inside a flat shape, counted in unit squares: cm², m², km². A rectangle $5$ wide and $7$ tall holds $7$ rows of $5$ squares — $35$ of them. That row-counting shortcut is the formula $A = l \times w$.

The formula survives fractions. A rectangle $\tfrac{1}{2}$ m by $\tfrac{1}{3}$ m holds exactly one tile of area $\tfrac{1}{6}$ m² — six such rectangles tile a unit square. Multiply the side lengths, whatever they are.

A **parallelogram** is a pushed-over rectangle. Slice the triangle off one slanted end and slide it to the other: a rectangle appears, with the SAME base and the SAME height. So $A = b \times h$ — base times height, exactly like the rectangle it secretly is.

The height is the PERPENDICULAR distance from base to opposite side — the shadow of a right-angle ruler, not the slanted side. The slant can be as long as it likes; only base and height enter the area.
lessons.keyIdea: Rectangle: $A = l \times w$. Parallelogram: $A = b \times h$ with $h$ PERPENDICULAR to the base — the slanted side never enters the formula.

facts.title: Rectangle
facts.explanation: Rows of unit squares: length times width.

facts.title: Parallelogram
facts.explanation: Slice-and-slide turns it into a rectangle with the same base and height.

facts.title: Height ⊥ base
facts.explanation: Height is measured at a right angle to the base.

workedExamples.statement: A rectangular garden is $12$ m long and $7$ m wide. Find its area.
workedExamples.solution: $A = l \times w = 12 \times 7 = 84$ m². Eighty-four one-meter squares of garden.

workedExamples.statement: A parallelogram has base $9$ cm, height $6$ cm, and slanted side $7.5$ cm. Find its area.
workedExamples.solution: Only base and height matter: $A = b \times h = 9 \times 6 = 54$ cm². The $7.5$ cm slant is a decoy — it never enters the formula.

workedExamples.statement: A sticker is $\tfrac{3}{4}$ cm by $\tfrac{2}{5}$ cm. Find its area.
workedExamples.solution: $A = \tfrac{3}{4} \times \tfrac{2}{5} = \tfrac{6}{20} = \tfrac{3}{10}$ cm². Fractional sides multiply like any others.

commonMistakes.text: Using the slanted side of a parallelogram as its height.
commonMistakes.correction: The height is the perpendicular distance between the bases. In a slanted parallelogram the side is LONGER than the height — using it overestimates the area.

commonMistakes.text: Confusing area with perimeter — adding sides when the question asks for space inside.
commonMistakes.correction: Perimeter adds the border ($2l + 2w$); area multiplies to count inside squares ($l \times w$). A $12 \times 7$ rectangle: perimeter $38$ m, area $84$ m² — different numbers, different units.

tryIt.statement: Find the area of a rectangle $15$ cm by $8$ cm.
A parallelogram has area $63$ m² and base $9$ m. Find its height.

steps.title: Rows × columns
beats: Area = how many unit squares fit inside.
A $5 \times 7$ rectangle: $7$ rows of $5$ squares.
$A = l \times w$ — multiplication IS fast counting.
Works for fractions too: $\tfrac12 \times \tfrac13 = \tfrac16$ of a square meter.

steps.title: Slice and slide
beats: A parallelogram leans — but lean is a costume.
Slice the triangle off one end; slide it to the other end.
A rectangle appears: same base $b$, same height $h$.
So parallelogram area $= b \times h$. The slant never mattered.

steps.title: Dodge the decoy
steps.prompt: A parallelogram has base $8$, height $5$, slant side $6$. Its area is…

steps.title: Formula forwards and backwards
steps.intro: Multiply for area; divide to recover a side.

examples.prompt: A rectangle has area $72$ cm² and length $9$ cm. Width?
Parallelogram: base $\tfrac{5}{2}$ cm, height $\tfrac{4}{5}$ cm. Area?

problems.prompt: A parallelogram's area is $54$ with height $6$. Its base is…
Doubling BOTH sides of a rectangle multiplies its area by…
problems.explanation: $(2l)(2w) = 4lw$ — twice the rows AND twice the columns.

steps.title: Area is older than algebra
steps.body: Babylonian clay tablets from 1800 BCE — three thousand years before algebra notation — record land areas computed as length × width for tax purposes. Area formulas may be humanity's oldest applied mathematics: the tax collector arrived before the mathematician.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Area counts unit squares; rectangle $A = lw$.
Parallelogram = pushed-over rectangle: $A = bh$.
Height ⊥ base — the slanted side is a decoy.
Backwards: divide area by one dimension to find the other.

lessons.title: Area of Triangles
lessons.concreteComparison: Fold a square slice of bread corner to corner and you hold two identical triangles — each exactly half the slice. Every triangle is half of some parallelogram, and that one fold is the whole formula: $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$.
lessons.objective: Find areas of triangles with $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$, choosing a correct base–height pair, including right, acute, and obtuse triangles.
concept: Take any triangle, rotate a copy $180°$, and snap the two together along a side: a parallelogram appears with the same base $b$ and height $h$. The triangle is exactly half of it, so $A = \tfrac{1}{2} b h$.

The height is the perpendicular distance from the chosen base to the opposite vertex. In a right triangle the two legs are a ready-made base–height pair. In an obtuse triangle the height can land OUTSIDE the triangle — that's fine; it is still the perpendicular distance.

Any of the three sides can be the base, each with its own matching height — all three pairs give the same area. Choose the pair you actually know; never pair one side's base with another side's height.
Working backwards: from $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$, a known area and base give the height, $h = \dfrac{2A}{b}$.
lessons.keyIdea: A triangle is half a parallelogram: $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$, with $h$ perpendicular to the CHOSEN base (it may fall outside an obtuse triangle).

facts.title: Triangle area
facts.explanation: Half of the parallelogram made by doubling the triangle.

facts.title: Right triangle
facts.explanation: The legs are a built-in base–height pair.

facts.title: Backwards
facts.explanation: Double the area, divide by the base.

workedExamples.statement: Find the area of a triangle with base $10$ cm and height $7$ cm.
A right triangle has legs $9$ m and $12$ m, and its longest side is $15$ m. Find the area.
workedExamples.solution: The legs meet at the right angle, so they are base and height: $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 12 = 54$ m². The $15$ m side is the hypotenuse — not needed.

workedExamples.statement: A triangular sail has area $30$ m² and base $12$ m. How tall is it?

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ — computing $b \times h$ and reporting a parallelogram's area.
commonMistakes.correction: The triangle is HALF the parallelogram. $b \times h$ counts both copies; divide by 2: $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$.

commonMistakes.text: Pairing a base with a height that belongs to a different side.
commonMistakes.correction: Each base has its own perpendicular height. Mixing side 10's base with side 8's height computes the area of nothing. Use a matched pair.

tryIt.statement: Find the area of a triangle with base $16$ and height $5$.
A triangle has area $24$ cm² and height $6$ cm. Find the base.

steps.title: Two triangles make a parallelogram
beats: Copy the triangle, spin the copy $180°$, snap them together.
Result: a parallelogram, base $b$, height $h$, area $bh$.
Your triangle is exactly half: $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$.
One picture, whole formula.

steps.title: Outside is allowed
beats: Height = perpendicular distance from base line to the far vertex.
Right triangle: the two legs already meet at $90°$ — free pair.
Obtuse triangle: the perpendicular can land OUTSIDE the shape.
Still the height. The formula doesn't flinch.

steps.title: Don't skip the half
steps.explanation: $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 9 = 27$. Without the half you'd get the parallelogram's $54$.

examples.prompt: A triangle's sides are $5, 12, 13$ with the right angle between $5$ and $12$. Area?
steps: Legs $5$ and $12$ form the right angle — matched pair.

examples.prompt: A triangle with base $\tfrac{8}{3}$ cm and height $\tfrac{9}{4}$ cm. Area?

problems.prompt: Area of a triangle with base $20$ and height $3$?
Two triangles share the same base and equal heights. Their areas are…
problems.explanation: Area depends only on base and height — shape can differ, area can't.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has legs $7$ and $8$. Its area is…

steps.title: Egypt's rope-stretchers
steps.body: After each Nile flood erased field boundaries, Egyptian surveyors called harpedonaptai — “rope-stretchers” — re-measured triangular plots with knotted ropes so taxes stayed fair. Greek visitors were so impressed they named the science after it: geo (earth) + metria (measure). Triangle area fed a civilization.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Triangle = half a parallelogram: $A = \tfrac{1}{2}bh$.
Base and height must be a matched, perpendicular pair.
Right triangles: legs are the pair; obtuse: height may fall outside.
Backwards: $h = 2A/b$, $b = 2A/h$.

lessons.title: Trapezoids & Composite Figures
lessons.concreteComparison: An L-shaped living room doesn't come with its own formula — and doesn't need one. Slice it into two rectangles, add the pieces, done. Composite figures are just familiar shapes wearing a trench coat, and a trapezoid is the same idea with a built-in shortcut.
lessons.objective: Find areas of trapezoids with $A = \tfrac{(b_1 + b_2)}{2} \cdot h$, and of composite figures by slicing-and-adding or surrounding-and-subtracting.
concept: A **trapezoid** has two parallel sides — the bases $b_1$ and $b_2$ — a height $h$ between them, and a shortcut: two copies snap together into a parallelogram of base $b_1 + b_2$ and height $h$. Half of that: $A = \dfrac{b_1 + b_2}{2} \cdot h$ — the AVERAGE of the bases times the height.

A **composite figure** is built from shapes you already know. Strategy one — **slice and add**: cut it into rectangles and triangles along natural lines, find each piece, add. Strategy two — **surround and subtract**: box the whole figure in one big rectangle and subtract the notches.
Both strategies must agree — computing an L-shape both ways is a free error check. Choose whichever needs fewer pieces; for shapes with a bite taken out, subtracting is usually faster.
Bookkeeping wins these problems: label every length before computing, find missing sides from the ones given (parallel edges must total the same), and keep one piece = one line of arithmetic.
lessons.keyIdea: Trapezoid: average the parallel bases, times height. Composite figures: slice-and-add, or surround-and-subtract — both roads must reach the same area.

facts.title: Trapezoid
facts.explanation: Average of the two parallel sides, times the height between them.

facts.title: Slice & add
facts.explanation: Cut along natural lines into rectangles/triangles.

facts.title: Surround & subtract
facts.explanation: Box the figure; subtract what isn't there.

workedExamples.statement: A trapezoid has parallel sides $8$ cm and $14$ cm, with height $5$ cm. Find its area.
An L-shaped room is a $6$ m × $4$ m rectangle with a $3$ m × $2$ m rectangle attached below its left half. Find the floor area.
workedExamples.solution: Slice and add: $6 \times 4 = 24$ m² plus $3 \times 2 = 6$ m², total $30$ m². Check by surrounding: the bounding box is $6 \times 6 = 36$; the missing notch is $3 \times 2 = 6$; $36 - 6 = 30$ m². ✓ Both roads agree.

workedExamples.statement: A rectangular banner $10$ dm × $6$ dm has a triangular flap (base $6$ dm, height $4$ dm) attached to its right edge. Total area?
workedExamples.solution: Rectangle: $10 \times 6 = 60$ dm². Triangle: $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 4 = 12$ dm². Total $= 60 + 12 = 72$ dm².

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to halve the sum of the bases in the trapezoid formula — computing $(b_1+b_2) \cdot h$.
commonMistakes.correction: Two trapezoid copies make ONE parallelogram of base $b_1 + b_2$; your trapezoid is half of it. Average the bases first: $\frac{b_1+b_2}{2} \cdot h$.

commonMistakes.text: Double-counting the shared edge region when slicing a composite figure.
commonMistakes.correction: Slices must tile the figure — no overlaps, no gaps. Draw the cut line, label each piece's own dimensions, and make sure every part of the floor belongs to exactly one piece.

tryIt.statement: A trapezoid has bases $9$ and $15$ and height $6$. Find the area.
A $12 \times 9$ rectangle has a $4 \times 3$ rectangular bite removed from one corner. Find the remaining area.

steps.title: Trapezoid = half a parallelogram, again
beats: Two trapezoid copies, one flipped, snap into a parallelogram.
Its base: $b_1 + b_2$. Its height: the same $h$.
So trapezoid $A = \frac{b_1+b_2}{2} \cdot h$ — average base × height.
Bases $8$ and $14$, height $5$: average $11$, area $55$.

steps.title: Shapes in a trench coat
steps.teach: This L-shape is two rectangles pretending to be one mystery. Watch it fall apart along the cut line — then add the two pieces.

steps.title: Average first
steps.explanation: Average base $= 8$; $8 \times 4 = 32$. Skipping the averaging gives the wrong $64$.

steps.title: Two roads, one area
steps.intro: Slice-add or surround-subtract — verify with both.

examples.prompt: A T-shape: top bar $8 \times 2$, stem $2 \times 5$ below its middle. Area?
A $9 \times 7$ plate with a $2 \times 2$ square hole. Remaining area?

steps.title: Carve it up
steps.intro: One piece, one line of arithmetic.

problems.prompt: An L-shape splits into rectangles $5\times3$ and $4\times2$. Total area?
A $10 \times 8$ rectangle loses a triangular corner of base $4$, height $3$. Remaining area?

options: computing the area a second way
problems.explanation: Slice-add and surround-subtract must agree — a free audit.

steps.title: City blocks are composite-figure homework
steps.body: Real estate surveyors compute odd-shaped lot areas exactly this way: decompose into triangles and trapezoids, sum the pieces, then re-verify with a different decomposition. A lot's legal record (its “metes and bounds”) is literally the instruction list for the slicing.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Trapezoid: average the parallel bases, multiply by height.
Composite figures: slice-and-add or surround-and-subtract.
The two roads must agree — use one to check the other.
Label every length before you compute.

lessons.title: Polygons on the Coordinate Plane
lessons.concreteComparison: City planners drop a building's corners onto a grid map as coordinates. The building's wall lengths aren't measured with a tape — they're SUBTRACTED: two corners on the same street differ by house numbers. Geometry on a grid turns rulers into arithmetic.
lessons.objective: Plot polygons from vertex coordinates, find horizontal/vertical side lengths by subtracting coordinates, and compute perimeters and areas.
concept: On the coordinate plane, a **horizontal** segment's length is the difference of the $x$-coordinates; a **vertical** segment's is the difference of the $y$-coordinates. From $(2, 3)$ to $(7, 3)$: $7 - 2 = 5$ units. Subtract, and take the positive difference — lengths are never negative.

A polygon given by its vertices becomes concrete once plotted: connect corners in order, and the grid reveals which sides are horizontal or vertical. Perimeter = add the side lengths; for a rectangle with grid-aligned sides, area = (difference of $x$'s) × (difference of $y$'s).
Right triangles with grid-aligned legs read straight off the coordinates: the legs' lengths are the coordinate differences, and $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{leg} \cdot \text{leg}$.
Stranger polygons yield to last lesson's tools — slice into grid-aligned rectangles and triangles, or box the shape and subtract corners. Coordinates make every needed length a subtraction.
lessons.keyIdea: Grid-aligned lengths are coordinate differences: horizontal $\to$ subtract $x$'s, vertical $\to$ subtract $y$'s. Then perimeter and area proceed as usual.

facts.title: Grid rectangle
facts.explanation: Width and height come straight from the vertices.

workedExamples.statement: Find the length of the segment from $(-3, 4)$ to $(5, 4)$.
workedExamples.solution: Same $y$-coordinate → horizontal. Length $= 5 - (-3) = 8$ units. Crossing the $y$-axis changes nothing: subtract and keep it positive.

workedExamples.statement: A rectangle has vertices $(1, 2)$, $(7, 2)$, $(7, 6)$, $(1, 6)$. Find its perimeter and area.
workedExamples.solution: Width $= 7 - 1 = 6$; height $= 6 - 2 = 4$. Perimeter $= 2(6 + 4) = 20$ units; area $= 6 \times 4 = 24$ square units.

workedExamples.statement: Triangle with vertices $(0, 0)$, $(6, 0)$, $(0, 5)$: find its area.
workedExamples.solution: Two sides run along the axes: legs $6 - 0 = 6$ and $5 - 0 = 5$, meeting at the right angle at the origin. $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 5 = 15$ square units.

commonMistakes.text: Subtracting coordinates across an axis and dropping the sign work: from $(-3, 4)$ to $(5, 4)$ getting $5 - 3 = 2$.
commonMistakes.correction: Subtract the actual coordinates: $5 - (-3) = 8$. A negative coordinate is not a positive one in disguise.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing up which coordinates to subtract — using $x$'s for a vertical side.
commonMistakes.correction: Horizontal sides (same $y$): subtract $x$'s. Vertical sides (same $x$): subtract $y$'s. Check which coordinate the endpoints SHARE first.

tryIt.statement: How long is the segment from $(4, -2)$ to $(4, 9)$?
A rectangle has vertices $(-2, 1)$, $(3, 1)$, $(3, 4)$, $(-2, 4)$. Find its area.

steps.title: Grid lengths are differences
beats: Two corners on the same horizontal: length $= x$-difference.
Same vertical: length $= y$-difference.
$(2,3)$ to $(7,3)$: $7-2 = 5$. No ruler required.
Negative coordinates included: $5 - (-3) = 8$.

steps.title: Plot, connect, measure
beats: Plot each vertex; connect them in the listed order.
Spot the grid-aligned sides — their lengths are subtractions.
Rectangle: area $= \Delta x \cdot \Delta y$; triangle with axis legs: $\tfrac{1}{2}$ product.
Weird shape? Slice or box it, like last lesson.

steps.title: Vertex arithmetic
steps.intro: Share a coordinate → subtract the other.

steps: Right angle at $(2,1)$ where they meet.

problems.prompt: Vertical segment from $(3, -4)$ to $(3, 2)$ has length…
A square has vertices $(0,0)$ and $(4,0)$ as one side. Its area is…

steps.title: The fly on Descartes' ceiling
steps.body: Legend says René Descartes invented coordinates while sick in bed, watching a fly and wondering how to describe its position — two numbers, distance from each wall, would do it. True or not, the 1637 idea fused algebra and geometry: every shape became equations, every length a subtraction.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Grid lengths are coordinate differences — subtract, stay positive.
Shared $y$ → horizontal (subtract $x$'s); shared $x$ → vertical.
Grid rectangles and axis-leg triangles compute straight from vertices.
Odd polygons: slice or box, exactly like composite figures.

lessons.title: Volume of Rectangular Prisms
lessons.concreteComparison: Packing a moving box with 1-dm sugar cubes: a layer of 4 × 3 = 12 cubes covers the bottom, and 5 layers fill the box — 60 cubes. Volume is layer × layers, and the formula $V = lwh$ is just that packing plan written small.
lessons.objective: Find volumes of rectangular prisms with $V = lwh$ (including fractional edge lengths), and recover a missing edge from a known volume.
concept: **Volume** measures the space inside a solid, counted in unit cubes: cm³, m³. For a rectangular prism, one floor layer holds $l \times w$ cubes and there are $h$ layers: $V = l \times w \times h$. Equivalently $V = Bh$ — base area times height.

Fractional edges work the same way, with smaller cubes. A prism $\tfrac{5}{2} \times 2 \times \tfrac{3}{2}$ m packs perfectly with half-meter cubes ($\tfrac{1}{8}$ m³ each): $5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60$ little cubes $\times \tfrac{1}{8} = 7\tfrac{1}{2}$ m³ — exactly $lwh$. Multiply the edges, fractions and all.
The three edges multiply in any order, so group the easy product first: $25 \times 7 \times 4 = (25 \times 4) \times 7 = 700$.
Backwards problems divide: a known volume and two edges give the third, $h = \dfrac{V}{l \cdot w}$ — how many layers the packing plan needs.
lessons.keyIdea: Volume packs unit cubes in layers: $V = lwh = Bh$. Fractional edges multiply the same way, and a missing edge is volume ÷ (product of the others).

facts.title: Prism volume
facts.explanation: Cubes per layer × number of layers.

facts.title: Base-area form
facts.explanation: Any face can be the “base”; multiply by the matching height.

facts.title: Missing edge
facts.explanation: Divide the volume by the two known edges' product.

workedExamples.statement: A storage box is $8$ dm long, $5$ dm wide, $6$ dm tall. Find its volume.
workedExamples.solution: $V = 8 \times 5 \times 6 = 240$ dm³. One layer holds $40$ cubes; six layers stack to $240$.

workedExamples.statement: A juice carton measures $\tfrac{3}{2}$ dm × $\tfrac{1}{2}$ dm × $2$ dm. Find its volume.
workedExamples.solution: $V = \tfrac{3}{2} \times \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2 = \tfrac{3}{2}$ dm³ — one and a half liters. Fractions multiply straight through.

workedExamples.statement: An aquarium holds $96$ liters ($96$ dm³) and its base is $8$ dm by $4$ dm. How deep is it?

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying only two of the three dimensions — reporting a floor's area as the box's volume.
commonMistakes.correction: Area covers the floor ($lw$, in square units); volume fills the room ($lwh$, in cubic units). Three lengths, three factors.

commonMistakes.text: Refusing fractional edges — rounding $2\tfrac{1}{2}$ m to $2$ or $3$ “so the cubes fit.”
commonMistakes.correction: Smaller cubes fit fractional edges perfectly, and the formula already accounts for them: $\tfrac{5}{2} \times 2 \times \tfrac{3}{2} = \tfrac{15}{2}$, no rounding.

tryIt.statement: Find the volume of a prism $7$ cm × $4$ cm × $9$ cm.
A cube has edges of $\tfrac{2}{3}$ m. Find its volume.

steps.title: Volume = packing plan
beats: Cover the floor: $l \times w$ unit cubes in one layer.
Stack $h$ layers.
$V = lwh$ — cubes per layer × layers.
Units go cubic: cm³, m³ — space, not surface.

steps.title: The prism rule
steps.teach: Spin the box. The bottom layer holds base-area worth of cubes, and the height counts the layers: volume is base × height, whichever face you call the base.

steps.title: Three factors
steps.explanation: $6 \times 3 \times 4 = 72$ cubic units — all three edges multiply.

steps.title: Forward, fractional, backward
steps.intro: Multiply edges; divide for missing ones.

examples.prompt: A box's volume is $180$ cm³ with a $6 \times 5$ base. Height?

steps.title: Fill 'er up
steps.intro: Cubic units; three dimensions every time.

problems.prompt: A cube of edge $4$ has volume…
Doubling a prism's height (only) does what to its volume?
problems.explanation: Twice the layers, same layer size: $l \cdot w \cdot 2h = 2\,lwh$.

steps.title: A million-dollar unit mistake
steps.body: In 1999, NASA's Mars Climate Orbiter burned up because one team computed in metric units and another in imperial — the numbers were right, the UNITS were not. Volume's cubic units triple the danger: mix up meters and decimeters and your answer is off by a factor of a thousand.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $V = lwh = Bh$: cubes per layer × layers.
Fractional edges multiply straight through.
Missing edge $=$ volume ÷ product of the other two.
Cubic units always — check them like cargo.

lessons.title: Nets & Surface Area
lessons.concreteComparison: Flatten a cereal box along its seams and it becomes one flat cross-shaped piece of cardboard — the box's net. How much cardboard does the box use? Exactly the net's area. Surface area is a wrapping question, answered by unfolding.
lessons.objective: Unfold prisms into nets, and compute surface areas as sums of face areas — including the rectangular-prism shortcut $SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$.
concept: A **net** is a solid unfolded flat along its edges — every face laid out as one connected piece. Fold it back up and the solid returns. A cube's net is six squares (in several valid arrangements); a rectangular prism's is six rectangles in three matching pairs.

**Surface area** is the total area of all faces — the net's area. It measures wrapping (cardboard, paint, gift paper) in SQUARE units, while volume measures filling in CUBIC units. One box, two different questions.
A rectangular prism's faces pair up: top/bottom ($lw$ each), front/back ($lh$ each), sides ($wh$ each). Sum: $SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$. For a cube of edge $s$: six identical squares, $SA = 6s^2$.
For any prism, the reliable method is inventory: list every face with its dimensions, compute each area, add. The formula is the inventory pre-added — when in doubt, take inventory.
lessons.keyIdea: Surface area = the net's area = sum of all face areas. Rectangular prism: $SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$; cube: $SA = 6s^2$. Square units, not cubic.

facts.title: Net
facts.explanation: Unfold along edges; refold to restore the solid.

facts.title: Rectangular prism
facts.explanation: Three face pairs: top/bottom, front/back, two sides.

workedExamples.statement: Find the surface area of a $5 \times 4 \times 3$ box.
How much wrapping paper covers a cube-shaped gift of edge $6$ cm (no overlap)?
A $10 \times 2 \times 2$ prism and a $4 \times 4 \times 2.5$ prism both have volume $40$. Compare their surface areas.
workedExamples.solution: First: $SA = 2(20 + 20 + 4) = 88$. Second: $SA = 2(16 + 10 + 10) = 72$. Same volume, different skins — the long thin box needs $16$ more square units of cardboard. Compact shapes wrap cheaper.

commonMistakes.text: Counting only the three visible faces — computing $lw + lh + wh$ and forgetting each has a hidden twin.
commonMistakes.correction: Every face of a box has an opposite partner. Multiply the three-face sum by 2: $SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting surface area in cubic units, or volume in square units.
commonMistakes.correction: Surface area wraps (square units, cm²); volume fills (cubic units, cm³). The unit names the question you actually answered.

tryIt.statement: Find the surface area of a $7 \times 3 \times 2$ prism.
A cube has surface area $150$ cm². What is its edge length?

steps.title: Nets: solids gone flat
beats: Cut along some edges, unfold flat: that's a net.
Every face appears once, connected — fold it back, box returns.
A cube's net: six squares (11 different valid layouts!).
Net's area = the solid's surface area.

steps.title: The prism inventory
beats: Top & bottom: $l \times w$ each.
Front & back: $l \times h$ each.
Left & right: $w \times h$ each.
$SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$ — the inventory, pre-added.

steps.title: Wrap, don't fill
steps.prompt: Surface area of a cube with edge $3$?
steps.explanation: $6 \times 3^2 = 54$ square units. ($27$ is its volume — different question.)

examples.prompt: Paint covers $1$ m² per can-tenth. A $2 \times 2 \times 1$ m crate needs how many m² painted (all faces)?
Which needs more cardboard: a $8 \times 1 \times 1$ box or a $2 \times 2 \times 2$ cube (equal volume $8$)?
steps: The long box — $10$ more square units for the same volume.

problems.prompt: A net of a rectangular prism always contains…
problems.explanation: Opposite faces are twins: three pairs, six faces. (All six identical only for a cube.)

problems.prompt: Volume answers “how much fits”; surface area answers…
problems.explanation: Filling vs wrapping: cubic units vs square units.

steps.title: Bees are surface-area misers
steps.body: Honeycomb cells are hexagonal because hexagons tile a plane using the least wall-wax for the space enclosed — bees solved a surface-area-per-volume optimization millions of years before calculus. Mathematicians only PROVED the hexagon optimal in 1999 (the “Honeycomb Conjecture,” Thomas Hales).

steps.title: What you learned
points: Net = the solid unfolded; its area = surface area.
Prism: $SA = 2(lw + lh + wh)$; cube: $6s^2$.
Square units wrap; cubic units fill.
Same volume can wear very different skins — compact wraps cheapest.

practice.statement: A rectangular notice board is $9$ dm by $4$ dm. Find its area and perimeter.
practice.solution: Area $= 9 \times 4 = 36$ dm²; perimeter $= 2(9 + 4) = 26$ dm. Two questions, two units.

practice.statement: A parallelogram has base $\tfrac{7}{2}$ m and height $\tfrac{6}{7}$ m. Find its area.
A triangle has area $45$ cm² and base $10$ cm. Find its height.
A trapezoid-shaped field has parallel sides $30$ m and $50$ m, and the distance between them is $20$ m. Find its area.

An L-shaped hallway is a $7 \times 2$ m corridor with a $3 \times 2$ m branch attached below its left end (the whole shape fits in a $7 \times 4$ m box, leaving a $4 \times 2$ m empty notch). Find the floor area by slicing-and-adding, then verify by surrounding-and-subtracting.
practice.solution: Slice and add: $7 \times 2 = 14$ and $3 \times 2 = 6$, total $= 20$ m². Surround and subtract: the $7 \times 4$ box holds $28$ m²; removing the empty $4 \times 2 = 8$ m² notch leaves $28 - 8 = 20$ m². Both roads agree. ✓
Legs along grid lines: base $= 9 - 1 = 8$, height $= 8 - 2 = 6$, right angle at $(1,2)$. $A = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 6 = 24$ square units.

practice.statement: A fish tank is $6$ dm long and $3$ dm wide and holds $54$ liters when full ($1$ L $= 1$ dm³). Find its height.
Find the volume and surface area of a $4 \times 3 \times \tfrac{5}{2}$ box.
practice.solution: Volume: $4 \times 3 \times \tfrac{5}{2} = 30$ cubic units. Surface area: $2\!\left(12 + 10 + \tfrac{15}{2}\right) = 2 \times \tfrac{59}{2} = 59$ square units.

testYourself.statement: A parallelogram has base $13$ cm, height $8$ cm, and slant side $10$ cm. Find its area.
testYourself.solution: $A = bh = 13 \times 8 = 104$ cm² — the slant side is a decoy.

testYourself.statement: Find the area of a triangle with base $\tfrac{9}{2}$ m and height $\tfrac{8}{3}$ m.
A trapezoid has bases $11$ and $17$ and area $84$. Find its height.
A rectangle has vertices $(-4, -1)$, $(2, -1)$, $(2, 3)$, $(-4, 3)$. Find its perimeter and area.
testYourself.solution: Width $= 2 - (-4) = 6$; height $= 3 - (-1) = 4$. Perimeter $= 2(6+4) = 20$ units; area $= 24$ square units.

testYourself.statement: A cube has volume $\tfrac{27}{8}$ cm³. Find its edge length and surface area.
testYourself.solution: Edge: $s^3 = \tfrac{27}{8} \Rightarrow s = \tfrac{3}{2}$ cm. Surface area: $6s^2 = 6 \times \tfrac{9}{4} = \tfrac{27}{2} = 13.5$ cm².

testYourself.statement: A gift box is $8$ cm × $5$ cm × $4$ cm. How much space is inside, and how much paper wraps it exactly?
testYourself.solution: Volume: $8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160$ cm³. Surface area: $2(40 + 32 + 20) = 184$ cm². Filling vs wrapping — cubic vs square units.
